title: Term Insurance — Why You Need It
meta_description: Term insurance is the purest life cover — high coverage at low premium. Learn how much cover you need, best plans, and common mistakes to avoid.

hero.one_liner: If something happens to you, will your family survive financially? Term insurance pays a large sum to your family for a very small premium.

sections.heading: What is Term Insurance?
content: Term insurance is pure life insurance — if you die during the policy term, your family gets a large lump sum (₹50 lakh to ₹2 crore). If you survive the term, you get nothing back. That's what makes it cheap — you're only paying for risk coverage, not investment.

A 30-year-old non-smoker can get ₹1 crore life cover for just ₹700-1,000 per month. Compare this to LIC endowment plans that charge ₹5,000-10,000/month for ₹10-20 lakh cover. Term insurance gives 10× more coverage at a fraction of the cost.

Term insurance is for anyone whose family depends on their income — salaried employees, self-employed professionals, or business owners. If you died tomorrow, could your family pay EMIs, children's school fees, and daily expenses? Term insurance ensures they can.

sections.heading: How Much Cover Do You Need?

rows.label: Add for goals
rows.value: Children's education (₹20-50L per child) + spouse's retirement needs

rows.label: Subtract
rows.value: Existing savings, EPF balance, other insurance

steps.title: Compare plans online
steps.description: Use PolicyBazaar, InsuranceDekho, or go directly to insurer websites (HDFC Life, ICICI Prudential, Max Life, Tata AIA). Compare premium, claim settlement ratio, and riders.

steps.title: Choose coverage and term
steps.description: Cover: 10-15× annual income. Term: until age 60-65. Pay attention to claim settlement ratio — choose insurers with 98%+ CSR.

steps.title: Add critical illness rider
steps.description: For ₹200-400 extra per month, add a critical illness rider. It pays a lump sum if you're diagnosed with cancer, heart attack, or stroke — while you're still alive. Very important addition.

steps.title: Fill application honestly
steps.description: Disclose all medical history truthfully — smoking, drinking, pre-existing conditions, family history. Lying can cause claim rejection when your family needs it most.

steps.title: Complete medical tests
steps.description: For covers above ₹50 lakh, the insurer sends someone for blood tests and basic medical examination. It's free and done at your home. Results go directly to the insurer.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes to Avoid
content: Don't buy LIC endowment, money-back, or ULIP plans thinking they're better because 'you get money back.' These give ₹10-25 lakh cover at ₹5,000-10,000/month premium. A term plan gives ₹1 crore cover at ₹800/month. Buy term insurance for protection, invest separately in mutual funds for wealth creation.

Don't delay buying. Every year you wait, the premium increases by 5-8%. A 25-year-old pays ₹600/month for ₹1 crore. A 35-year-old pays ₹1,100/month for the same cover. Plus, if you develop health issues before buying, you may face loading (higher premium) or exclusions.
Don't under-insure. ₹25-50 lakh might seem like a lot, but think about 20 years of expenses, children's education, home loan EMIs, and inflation. ₹1 crore is the minimum for most families.

faqs.question: What if I survive the policy term?
faqs.answer: In a pure term plan, you get nothing back — that's why it's cheap. Some plans offer 'Return of Premium' (TROP) where you get all premiums back if you survive, but these cost 2-3× more. Pure term is better value — invest the premium difference in mutual funds.

faqs.question: Is the payout tax-free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The death benefit received by your nominee is completely tax-free under Section 10(10D). The premium you pay also qualifies for Section 80C deduction up to ₹1.5 lakh.

faqs.question: Can the insurer reject a claim?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you hid material information during application (pre-existing conditions, smoking habits, hazardous occupation). This is why honest disclosure is critical. After 3 years, the policy becomes incontestable in most cases.

faqs.question: Joint life or separate policies for couples?
faqs.answer: Separate policies are better. If one spouse dies in a joint policy, the cover ends. With separate policies, the surviving spouse still has their own cover. Also, claim process is simpler with individual policies.

faqs.question: When should I stop term insurance?
faqs.answer: When your family no longer depends on your income — children are independent, home loan is paid off, and you have enough retirement corpus. This is typically around age 55-60.

disclaimer: This article is for educational purposes only. Insurance needs vary by individual. Compare policies from multiple insurers and read the policy document carefully. Consult an insurance advisor for personalized recommendations.
